snappy: NVIDIA's previous-generation training GPU — the workhorse of 2020-2022 LLM development.
detail: Launched 2020. 40GB or 80GB of HBM2e, ~300 TFLOPS BF16. GPT-3, Stable Diffusion, original Llama were all trained on A100s. Now superseded by H100 and Blackwell but still common in production inference because supply is abundant and pricing is reasonable. Still widely available on cloud providers.

snappy: Umbrella term for any chip designed to speed up a specific workload — GPUs, TPUs, NPUs, ASICs all qualify.
detail: In AI context, "accelerator" usually means "the chip that does the matrix multiplies". The accelerator ecosystem is increasingly diverse: NVIDIA GPUs dominate training; AMD MI300 series is the credible alternative; Google TPU runs Gemini; Amazon Trainium/Inferentia targets AWS workloads; startups (Groq, Cerebras, Sambanova) chase specific bottlenecks like inference latency.

snappy: The standard optimizer used to train almost every modern neural network.
detail: Adam tracks both the running average of gradients and their variance, then uses both to adapt the step size per parameter. AdamW, a small variation that handles regularisation cleanly, is the current default for LLM training. Introduced in 2014 and never seriously displaced — competitors like Adafactor and Lion exist but Adam/AdamW remains the safe choice.

snappy: Apple's family of CPUs/SoCs powering modern Macs (and iPad Pro) — with the Neural Engine doing the on-device AI.
detail: M1 (2020) through M4 (2024) and beyond. Unified memory architecture means the GPU and CPU share the same pool — important for large local LLM inference. Apple Intelligence's on-device 3B model runs natively. The M-series Max and Ultra variants are competitive with discrete GPUs for inference of mid-sized models.

snappy: Apple's on-device NPU, embedded in every iPhone, iPad, and Mac since 2017.
detail: Optimised for inference of small-to-medium models at very low power. The ANE in A18 Pro and M4 chips delivers 35-38 TFLOPS. Apple Intelligence (the on-device part) runs on the ANE. Developers access it indirectly through Core ML, the Apple framework for on-device machine learning.

snappy: A chip designed to do exactly one job extremely well — versus a CPU or GPU which is built to do anything.
detail: AI ASICs like Google TPUs, Amazon Trainium/Inferentia, and Groq's LPU sacrifice generality for efficiency on the specific patterns of neural network compute. They're typically 5-10× faster on their target workload than a general GPU at the same power. The downside: long design cycles (2-3 years) and inflexibility if the AI architecture shifts.

snappy: How a model decides which parts of the input matter most for what it's about to produce.
detail: Originally introduced in 2014 to fix a bottleneck in translation models, attention lets a network decide on the fly which parts of the input to look at, rather than processing them in a fixed order. The 2017 paper "Attention Is All You Need" showed that this trick alone — without the older sequential machinery — could match or beat the state of the art, kicking off the Transformer era that powers ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini.

snappy: Google Cloud's custom ARM-based CPU — Google joining AWS Graviton and Microsoft Cobalt in the cloud-CPU race.
detail: Built on ARM Neoverse cores. Targets cloud workloads that don't need the kind of single-thread performance x86 chips deliver — web servers, databases, data processing. Not an AI accelerator, but matters for the cloud-AI story because it reduces dependency on Intel/AMD for the host side of TPU-powered systems.

snappy: NVIDIA's successor to the H100 — twice the transistors via two dies stitched together.
detail: Announced March 2024, shipping in 2025. Uses TSMC's CoWoS packaging to join two reticle-sized dies into one logical chip with 192GB of HBM3e. Roughly 2.5× the FP8 compute of an H100, much higher memory bandwidth, and native FP4 for inference. Sold mostly as part of GB200 systems combining it with the Grace CPU.

snappy: The algorithm that figures out how much each parameter in a deep network contributed to the error.
detail: Without backprop, training deep networks is impossible. The trick is to use calculus's chain rule to propagate the error signal backward from the output through every layer, computing a gradient for each parameter. Introduced in the 1980s but only became practical for large networks once GPUs made the linear algebra fast enough. Every training run of every modern AI model runs backprop on every batch.

snappy: A group of examples processed together in one training step — much more efficient than doing them one at a time.
detail: Modern GPUs are designed for parallel computation; processing one example at a time would waste most of their capacity. A batch is a stack of (say) 32, 256, or 1024 examples fed through together. Larger batches make training more stable but require more memory. Frontier models often combine "micro-batches" with gradient accumulation to fit huge effective batch sizes into limited GPU memory.

snappy: Decoding strategy that keeps several candidate sequences alive at once and picks the best at the end.
detail: Instead of committing to one next token, beam search expands the top-K continuations of every partial sequence at each step, keeping a fixed-size "beam" of best candidates. Used heavily in translation and summarisation, where there's a clear single "correct" answer. Less useful for open-ended chat, where sampling produces more interesting outputs.

snappy: 16-bit format with the same exponent range as FP32 but fewer fraction bits — the modern default for training.
detail: Designed by Google for TPUs. 8-bit exponent (same range as FP32), 7-bit fraction (less precise than FP16). The wider exponent range means BF16 rarely underflows — no loss scaling needed. Now the standard format for training large language models on both GPUs and TPUs.

snappy: Common tokeniser training method — starts with single characters and repeatedly merges the most frequent pair into a new token.
detail: Begin with single bytes as the token vocabulary. Find the most common adjacent pair, glue them together into a new token. Repeat until you hit the target vocabulary size. GPT, Llama, and Mistral all use BPE variants. Its strength is simplicity and coverage: any input, in any language or alphabet, can be tokenised.

snappy: Model that links images and text in a shared space — lets you search images by description, or steer image generators with words.
detail: OpenAI's 2021 CLIP trained a vision network and a text network side-by-side on 400 million image-caption pairs, with a loss that pulls matching pairs together and pushes mismatched pairs apart. The resulting shared space enables zero-shot image classification, text-based search, and prompt conditioning for image generators (Stable Diffusion uses CLIP for the text input).

snappy: A workload limited by the speed of math operations, not memory access.
detail: Large-batch training is often compute-bound: GPUs can keep their math units fed because they reuse weights across many examples. Most LLM inference is the opposite — memory-bound, because each new token requires reading the whole model. Knowing which regime you're in determines which optimisations help (more compute? more bandwidth? better caching?).

snappy: Anthropic's approach to alignment — the model is shown a written list of principles and trained to critique itself against them.
detail: Instead of asking humans to rate every response, the model is given a "constitution" — a list of principles like "be helpful", "don't help with harm", "avoid stereotypes". The model generates a response, then critiques and rewrites it against those principles. Used to train Claude. The aim is to make alignment more transparent (the principles are written down) and scalable (humans don't need to rate every output).

snappy: The maximum amount of text a model can hold in mind at once, measured in tokens.
detail: Bounded by attention's quadratic memory cost. Early GPT models had 2,000–4,000-token windows; GPT-4 expanded to 128,000; Claude 3.5 to 200,000; Gemini 1.5 to 1 million-plus. Longer windows let you stuff more context (whole books, long codebases) but inference cost rises with window length, and models often struggle to use very long contexts evenly — the "lost in the middle" effect.

snappy: Inference scheduling where finished requests are replaced mid-batch — keeps the GPU full when requests have different lengths.
detail: Standard batching waits for the longest request in a batch to finish before starting new ones, wasting compute. Continuous batching adds new prompts to the batch as soon as slots open, dramatically improving throughput for chat-like workloads with varying response lengths. The headline innovation behind vLLM and now standard across inference engines.

snappy: Type of network built for images — it slides small pattern detectors across the input to pick up local features.
detail: CNNs dominated computer vision from AlexNet (2012) through the late 2010s — face recognition, object detection, image classification. They're naturally suited to images because the same small filter can find an edge or a texture anywhere in the picture. Vision Transformers have since matched or beaten them on most benchmarks, but CNNs are still the default for vision on phones and small chips.

snappy: The general-purpose processor at the heart of every computer — good at everything, great at nothing in particular.
detail: CPUs run the operating system, schedule tasks, and handle the kinds of branchy logic GPUs are bad at. Modern server CPUs (Intel Xeon, AMD EPYC, AWS Graviton) have 32-128 cores. AI workloads mostly run on GPUs but the CPU still hosts the show: loading data, coordinating between accelerators, running pre/post-processing. Inference of small models can run perfectly well on CPU.

snappy: The general-purpose compute units in NVIDIA GPUs — handle everything that isn't a tensor-core matrix multiply.
detail: Each modern NVIDIA GPU has thousands of CUDA cores running in parallel (an H100 has over 16,000). They run regular floating-point arithmetic and are what gets used for the parts of a workload that aren't matrix multiplies — activations, normalisations, indexing, custom kernels. Tensor cores do the heavy lifting; CUDA cores do everything else.

snappy: Each GPU holds a full copy of the model and works on a different slice of the batch — the simplest parallel setup.
detail: After every step, GPUs exchange gradients so they stay synchronised. Easy to implement and scales linearly up to maybe 100 GPUs. Beyond that, the communication overhead and memory cost of replicating the model become prohibitive — bigger models switch to tensor/pipeline parallelism or sharded variants like FSDP.

snappy: The part of a model that writes its output one word at a time, each word built on what's come before.
detail: Decoders can only look backward, never forward — necessary for left-to-right generation. Today's chatbots (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, Grok) are all decoder-only Transformers; they've absorbed the encoder's job into a single stack. This makes them simpler to train at scale and is one reason the decoder-only architecture has dominated since GPT-3.

snappy: The general process of turning a model's predictions into an output sequence.
detail: Covers everything from greedy decoding through beam search and various sampling strategies. The choice of decoding strategy can have a bigger effect on output quality than people realise — the same model can feel completely different with different decoding settings. Modern chat APIs expose temperature, top-p, and a few other dials.

snappy: NVIDIA's pre-built AI server systems — racks of GPUs with networking and storage included.
detail: DGX H100 is a single chassis with 8 H100s connected by NVSwitch. DGX SuperPOD links many DGX nodes into a multi-thousand-GPU cluster. NVIDIA also sells DGX Cloud, which rents these systems by the hour. The DGX brand carries premium pricing but takes the integration headaches off the customer.

snappy: Generative model that learns to turn random static into a real-looking image by removing noise step by step.
detail: Training works by taking an image, adding random noise until it's pure static, and teaching the model to reverse one step of that process. At generation time, the model starts from noise and denoises iteratively to produce a fresh image. DALL-E 3, Stable Diffusion, Flux, Midjourney, and OpenAI's video model Sora all work this way. Diffusion is now spreading to audio, video, and even text.

snappy: Training a small model to mimic the outputs of a large one — cheaper to run, often surprisingly good.
detail: The large "teacher" model produces outputs (or probability distributions) on a big set of prompts. The small "student" is trained to match them. Distilled models capture most of the teacher's capabilities at a fraction of the cost. GPT-4o mini, Claude Haiku, and Gemini Flash are all distilled from larger siblings. Phi-3 is a notable open-source example.

snappy: Tesla's custom AI training accelerator, built specifically for self-driving and humanoid-robot training.
detail: Each Dojo "D1" chip is custom silicon manufactured by TSMC. Cabinets combine many of them into "tiles" and then full systems. Tesla deploys Dojo as part of its training infrastructure for FSD and Optimus alongside large NVIDIA H100/B200 clusters. Existence and roadmap occasionally clouded by mixed signals from Tesla leadership.

snappy: Simpler alternative to RLHF that skips the explicit reward model — just adjusts the model directly from preference data.
detail: RLHF is complex: train reward model, then run reinforcement learning on top. DPO collapses this into a single supervised loss that pushes the model toward preferred responses and away from rejected ones. Released by Stanford in 2023 and quickly adopted across the industry — Mistral, Meta, and many open-source projects use DPO instead of full RLHF. Often gets comparable results with much less engineering complexity.

snappy: Quintillion (10¹⁸) math operations per second — the scale of modern frontier training infrastructure.
detail: 1 EFLOPS = 1,000 PFLOPS. Frontier model training runs at multi-EFLOPS aggregate compute. NVIDIA's GB200 NVL72 rack is rated at roughly 1.4 EFLOPS at FP8. The largest training clusters announced (xAI's Colossus, Meta's training infrastructure, Microsoft/OpenAI's Stargate) reach 10+ EFLOPS.

snappy: The part of a model that reads the whole input upfront and prepares it for further processing.
detail: Encoders work bidirectionally — every word can see every other word — making them well-suited to understanding tasks like classification and search. BERT, Google's 2018 model that became the foundation for search ranking, is encoder-only. In encoder-decoder models like T5 or the original Transformer, the encoder digests the input before handing off to a decoder that writes the output.

snappy: Two-part design where one network reads the input and another writes the output.
detail: The original 2017 Transformer was encoder-decoder, built for translation. Modern translation models, summarisers, and speech recognisers (Whisper from OpenAI) still use this shape because the input-to-output mapping is clear. Decoder-only models have taken over chat and writing assistants, where the boundary between reading and writing is blurrier.

snappy: One full pass through the training dataset — sometimes models train for many, sometimes less than one.
detail: If your dataset has 1 million examples, one epoch means showing the model all 1 million once. Image models traditionally trained for 100+ epochs. Modern LLMs, with datasets in the trillions of tokens, often train for less than one epoch — they see most of the data only once. The shift came as both data and model size scaled up: with enough data, you don't need to revisit it.

snappy: Taking a pretrained model and adjusting it for a specific task, style, or behavior.
detail: After pretraining, models are fine-tuned to be useful for chat, follow instructions, or specialise in domains like medicine or coding. Fine-tuning is much cheaper than pretraining — hundreds or thousands of examples rather than billions — but it shapes a model's personality and competence at specific tasks. Most consumer LLMs you interact with (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) are fine-tuned versions of underlying base models.

snappy: Half-precision floating point — halves memory and roughly doubles throughput vs FP32.
detail: 5-bit exponent, 10-bit fraction. The exponent range is smaller than FP32, which can cause underflow during gradient computation — a problem solved by loss scaling. Used heavily in inference and in mixed-precision training. Widely supported by all modern GPUs.

snappy: The traditional precision for scientific computing — 8 bits of exponent, 23 bits of fraction, 1 sign bit.
detail: Roughly 7 decimal digits of precision. Used as the "reference" precision in machine learning, but expensive: every value takes 4 bytes. Modern training mostly uses FP16 or BF16 with FP32 only for accumulating gradients. Inference often pushes further down to FP8 or INT8 for speed.

snappy: Newest reduced-precision format — pushes training and inference speed further by halving precision again.
detail: Introduced by NVIDIA with the H100 (2022). Two variants: E4M3 (more precision) and E5M2 (more range). Used for inference today and increasingly for training too. The B200 and beyond support FP8 throughout. A 70B-parameter model in FP8 fits in 70GB of memory — half the FP16 size.

snappy: Reconfigurable chip that can be wired up in software to act like custom hardware — between a CPU and an ASIC.
detail: FPGAs sit between flexible CPUs and rigid ASICs: you can rewire them after manufacturing, but they're slower and more power-hungry than a dedicated ASIC doing the same job. Microsoft Azure famously deployed FPGAs at scale for Bing's search ranking. In AI, FPGAs are mostly used for prototyping new accelerator architectures before committing to silicon.

snappy: Data parallelism where each GPU holds only a slice of the model weights, fetching others on demand.
detail: Solves data parallelism's main problem (every GPU holds a full model copy). FSDP shards parameters, gradients, and optimizer state across all GPUs and gathers them just-in-time for each layer. Introduced by Meta in PyTorch; now the standard way to train large models on commodity clusters. Closely related to Microsoft's ZeRO.

snappy: Intel's AI accelerator line, inherited from the Habana Labs acquisition.
detail: Gaudi 2 (2022) and Gaudi 3 (2024). Targets training and inference at competitive cost vs NVIDIA. Strong adoption inside specific customers (Stability AI used Gaudi 2 for some workloads) but limited market share overall. Intel's Falcon Shores roadmap merges Gaudi with their Xeon CPU technology.

snappy: NVIDIA's combined system: two B200 GPUs paired with one Grace CPU on a single board.
detail: Sold as part of NVL36 (18 of these boards) and NVL72 (36 boards) rack-scale systems where all GPUs are connected via NVLink as if they were one giant accelerator. The GB200 NVL72 is rated around 1.4 EFLOPS of FP8 and is the workhorse of the 2025 training generation.

snappy: Two networks playing a game: one makes fake samples, the other tries to spot them. Both get better.
detail: Goodfellow and co-authors introduced GANs in 2014 and launched the modern generative-modelling era. StyleGAN dominated face generation in the late 2010s — those uncanny "this person does not exist" galleries. Training was famously brittle: modes would collapse, networks would diverge. Diffusion has largely replaced GANs at the frontier, but GANs still win on speed: one shot instead of many denoising steps.

snappy: NVIDIA's CPU+GPU "superchip" pairing one Grace ARM CPU with one H100 GPU on a single board.
detail: Connected by NVLink-C2C for 900 GB/s of CPU-GPU bandwidth — much higher than the PCIe link in a regular CPU+GPU pairing. Designed for memory-intensive workloads where you want fast access to host memory from the GPU. Now overshadowed by the GB200 (Grace+Blackwell) which is the same idea with the newer GPU.

snappy: Parallel hardware for training and inference
detail: Originally designed for graphics rendering, GPUs are now the dominant accelerator for deep learning. Their wide SIMD architecture matches the matrix-multiply pattern of neural networks. NVIDIA's CUDA ecosystem and HBM-equipped data-center GPUs (H100, B200) drive most frontier training runs.

snappy: The basic recipe for training neural networks — repeatedly nudge each parameter in the direction that reduces error.
detail: Start with random parameters. Show the model an example. Measure how wrong its prediction is. Calculate which way each parameter should move to reduce the error. Take a tiny step in that direction. Repeat billions of times. Every modern AI model — language, vision, robotics — is trained this way. Variants like SGD and Adam tweak the recipe for speed and stability.

snappy: Generating the highest-probability next token at every step — deterministic but often repetitive.
detail: The simplest decoding strategy: at each step, pick whichever token the model rates most likely. Always produces the same output for the same input. Tends to produce dull, repetitive text and gets stuck in loops on creative tasks. Useful for tasks where determinism matters (code completion, structured output) but mostly replaced by sampling-based methods for chat.

snappy: Inference-focused accelerator designed for absurdly low latency on language model serving.
detail: Groq's hardware doesn't try to compete with GPUs on flexibility — it's optimised for one thing: stream tokens out of an LLM as fast as physically possible. Demos consistently show 500-1,000 tokens/second on Llama models, several times faster than typical GPU serving. Sold via Groq's cloud API rather than direct hardware sales.

snappy: NVIDIA's data-centre GPU that powered the first wave of frontier LLM training and inference.
detail: Announced 2022. 80GB of HBM3, 3 TB/s bandwidth, FP8 support. Around 1,000 TFLOPS at FP8. The H100 became the world's most strategically important chip — Saudi Arabia bought 3,000, Meta hoarded hundreds of thousands, US export controls restricted China's access. Replaced by H200 (more memory) and then by Blackwell.

snappy: Mid-life upgrade to the H100 — same chip, more memory.
detail: Released late 2023. Same Hopper silicon as the H100 but upgraded to 141GB of HBM3e at 4.8 TB/s — enough to fit a 70B-parameter model in BF16 on a single GPU. Helped close the gap to Blackwell while Blackwell was in late development. Common in inference deployments where memory bandwidth is the bottleneck.

snappy: Using a trained model to produce outputs — the part of AI that happens every time you send a prompt.
detail: After a model is trained once (extremely expensive), it's run many billions of times to answer queries (still expensive, but per-query small). Inference dominates the running cost of consumer LLM services. Optimising inference — KV caching, quantization, batching, speculative decoding — is a major engineering frontier and the main job of inference frameworks like vLLM and TensorRT.

snappy: Amazon's inference-focused accelerator — sibling to Trainium, optimised for serving.
detail: Two generations: Inferentia 1 (2019) and Inferentia 2 (2023). Powers some Alexa and AWS-internal inference. The selling point is lower cost-per-token than NVIDIA GPUs for AWS-hosted models. Like Trainium, it's exclusive to AWS.

snappy: Fine-tuning a model specifically to follow instructions phrased as natural-language requests.
detail: Pioneered by Google's FLAN and OpenAI's InstructGPT — the model that became ChatGPT. The training data is a large collection of (instruction, response) pairs covering hundreds of task types: summarise this, translate that, answer this question. Instruction-tuned models follow prompts more reliably and respond well to zero-shot requests they've never seen before.

snappy: Integer format used for quantised inference — fast, memory-efficient, with manageable accuracy loss.
detail: Each value takes 1 byte and represents a number from -128 to 127. To represent neural network weights, an INT8 model also stores per-channel scale factors. INT8 inference is the workhorse of edge AI (phones, NPUs) and increasingly common in cloud inference too. INT4 pushes the trade-off further.

snappy: Intel's modern laptop chip family with integrated NPU — Lunar Lake (2024) hits 48 TOPS.
detail: Designed to qualify Intel laptops for Microsoft's Copilot+ branding. Combines CPU cores, integrated GPU, and a dedicated NPU on one die. Inference of small models (sub-10B parameters) runs comfortably on-device. Important for the consumer AI story but not relevant for frontier training.

snappy: NVIDIA's family of edge AI compute modules for robotics, drones, and embedded systems.
detail: Each Jetson module packs a small GPU plus ARM CPU cores in a single board, designed to run inference at the edge (no cloud needed). Jetson Orin Nano and AGX Orin are common; the newer Thor (2025) targets next-generation humanoid robots. Heavily used in autonomous machines and Edge AI products.

snappy: Memory of past computation that lets a language model generate each new token without redoing work for old ones.
detail: When generating word N, a Transformer would normally re-process all previous N-1 words. The KV cache stores intermediate results so each new word only adds one position's worth of computation. KV cache size grows linearly with context length and dominates inference memory at long contexts — a major reason 1M-token contexts are hard.

snappy: NVIDIA's inference-focused data-centre GPU based on the gaming Ada Lovelace architecture.
detail: 48GB of GDDR6 memory (cheaper, slower than HBM). Designed for inference and graphics workloads where memory bandwidth matters less than total memory capacity and price. Common in lower-cost cloud inference offerings. The L4 is a smaller, single-slot variant for edge servers.

snappy: How long a single request takes — usually split into time-to-first-token and time-per-token.
detail: Latency matters for interactive chat ("can you feel the lag?") more than for batch jobs. Time-to-first-token is dominated by prompt processing; subsequent tokens flow at the model's per-token speed. KV caching, prompt caching, and speculative decoding are all latency optimisations. Frontier APIs target 300–500ms time-to-first-token.

snappy: Diffusion that works on a compressed version of the image instead of raw pixels — much faster, almost no quality loss.
detail: A small autoencoder first compresses a 512×512 image down to a 64×64 mini-representation. Diffusion runs there, and the autoencoder expands it back to pixels at the end. The compute saving is 50× or more. Stable Diffusion's breakthrough was making this approach practical at consumer scale; almost every image generator since copies the trick.

snappy: A technique that stabilises training by rescaling the values flowing through each layer.
detail: Without layer norm (or its cousin batch norm), training deep networks is much harder — values can explode or vanish as they propagate through layers. Modern Transformers place layer norm in two spots per block ("pre-norm" placement is now standard). It's a small addition with an outsize stabilising effect, especially for the very large networks of modern LLMs.

snappy: The size of each step gradient descent takes — too big and training diverges, too small and it never finishes.
detail: The single most important hyperparameter in deep learning training. Modern setups use a "learning rate schedule" that varies the rate over the course of training: a brief warmup phase at the start, then a peak, then a slow decay (often cosine-shaped) toward zero. Get the schedule wrong and even a perfect dataset won't save you.

snappy: The raw, unnormalised scores a model outputs for each possible next token, before they're turned into probabilities.
detail: Logits are what comes out of the final layer of a language model — one number per vocabulary entry. Softmax converts them into a probability distribution. "Logit bias" APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic) let developers nudge specific tokens up or down before sampling, useful for forcing JSON output or banning specific words.

snappy: Efficient fine-tuning technique that adds small trainable matrices alongside the frozen original weights.
detail: Instead of fine-tuning all 70 billion parameters of a model, LoRA freezes them and adds tiny trainable "adapter" matrices alongside — typically 0.1-1% the size. Trains roughly 100× faster and the resulting adapters are small files you can swap in and out. Released by Microsoft Research in 2021 and now the dominant approach to customising open-source models. Hugging Face hosts thousands of community-trained LoRAs.

snappy: The formula a model uses to measure how wrong it is — what training tries to minimise.
detail: Different tasks use different losses: cross-entropy for language and classification, mean squared error for regression, contrastive losses for similarity learning. The choice of loss is one of the most important design decisions in ML — it defines what "good" means for the model. RLHF replaces a hand-coded loss with a learned reward model trained on human preferences.

snappy: Improved RNN with built-in machinery for remembering things across long sequences — the workhorse of language AI before Transformers.
detail: Introduced in 1997 by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, dominant for sequence tasks through the 2010s. The trick is a small set of internal switches that decide what to keep in memory, what to drop, and what to pass on at each step. Google Translate ran on LSTMs in production before the Transformer takeover, and LSTMs are still useful when memory and compute are tight — for example on phones.

snappy: Newer alternative to Transformers, designed for very long inputs — runs in linear time instead of quadratic.
detail: Released late 2023 by Gu and Dao. Mamba builds on "state space models": it keeps a compact rolling memory of the sequence so far, updated linearly per token. Its appeal is long-context efficiency — promising for tasks where Transformers' quadratic attention is prohibitive (long documents, video). Decoder-only Transformers remain dominant for general LLMs, but Mamba and its descendants are the leading alternative.

snappy: Generic term for the matrix-multiply hardware in modern chips — Apple, Intel, and ARM all use the phrase.
detail: Conceptually similar to NVIDIA's tensor cores: a block of silicon dedicated to multiplying small matrices together very quickly. Apple's Neural Engine, Intel's AMX (Advanced Matrix Extensions) on Sapphire Rapids and beyond, and ARM's SME (Scalable Matrix Extension) are examples. Every modern compute platform now ships some form of matrix engine.

snappy: How fast a chip can move data in and out of its memory — often the real bottleneck on AI workloads.
detail: Bigger than compute speed for many workloads. An H100 has 3.35 TB/s of HBM3 bandwidth; a B200 hits 8 TB/s with HBM3e. If your model's weights are 70GB and you need to read them once per token to generate output, memory bandwidth caps your token rate. This is why "memory-bound" is the most common bottleneck in LLM inference.

snappy: A workload limited by how fast you can pull data from memory, not by how fast the math units can compute.
detail: Single-user LLM inference is memory-bound: each new token requires loading the entire model from memory, but only does a small amount of math with it. Solutions: bigger batches (amortise the memory cost across many users), quantisation (less to load), HBM (faster memory), KV caching (less re-loading).

snappy: AMD's flagship AI accelerator — the only credible alternative to NVIDIA at frontier scale.
detail: Announced late 2023. 192GB of HBM3 (more than the H100), 5.3 TB/s bandwidth, competitive FP8 throughput. Microsoft, Meta, and Oracle have all deployed MI300X clusters. The MI325X and MI350X (2025) further close the gap with Blackwell. AMD's main weakness is software (ROCm vs CUDA) rather than silicon.

snappy: AMD's 2025 generation — moves to 3nm and adds first-class FP4/FP6 support.
detail: Built on TSMC's 3nm process with CDNA 4 architecture. Targets parity with NVIDIA Blackwell on FP8 inference and pushes ahead on memory capacity (288GB HBM3e). AMD's roadmap shows MI400 in 2026 with rack-scale interconnect to match NVIDIA's NVL systems.

snappy: Training or inference that uses 16-bit numbers for most operations and 32-bit only where needed.
detail: Halves memory and roughly doubles throughput on modern GPUs (which are designed for FP16/BF16). The trick is identifying which operations need full precision to stay numerically stable — usually a small subset including normalisation and the optimizer state. Standard in all modern LLM training and inference; BF16 is the dominant flavour for training.

snappy: Architecture that splits a model into many sub-networks and only activates a few of them for each input.
detail: MoE separates a model's total capacity (large) from its per-question compute cost (small). A router picks, say, 2 out of 64 experts for each word, so a 400-billion-parameter model might only fire 40 billion at a time. Mixtral, DeepSeek-V3, and (reportedly) GPT-4 all use MoE to grow capacity without proportionally growing inference cost.

snappy: Several attention operations running in parallel, each learning to spot a different kind of pattern.
detail: Instead of one attention pass, the model runs several at once with separate parameters. One head might track who-did-what; another might pick up rhyming patterns; another might handle long-range references. Frontier models use anywhere from 32 to 128 heads per layer. Recent variants — grouped-query attention, multi-query attention — share information across heads to cut inference cost.

snappy: Small AI accelerator built into phones, laptops, and edge devices — runs inference locally without going to the cloud.
detail: Apple's Neural Engine, Qualcomm's Hexagon NPU, Intel's NPU on Core Ultra chips, and the various Android NPUs all sit alongside the main CPU/GPU. They're tuned for inference (not training) and for low power. Used for on-device speech recognition, image processing, autocomplete, and small language models like Apple Intelligence's on-device LLM.

snappy: Umbrella term for fine-tuning methods that adjust only a small fraction of a model's parameters.
detail: Covers LoRA, QLoRA, prompt tuning, prefix tuning, adapter layers, and more. The shared goal: avoid the cost and disk space of full fine-tuning while preserving most of the quality. PEFT methods are essential for adapting frontier models to specific tasks at a small lab or company that can't afford to retrain the whole thing. Hugging Face's PEFT library is the standard implementation.

snappy: Unit for measuring how much compute went into a training run — one petaflop sustained for one day.
detail: GPT-3's training was around 3,000 PFLOP-days. GPT-4 was rumoured at 200,000+. Modern frontier models are reported in millions of PFLOP-days. The metric is useful because it captures the total work done, regardless of how many chips ran for how long. Roughly 1 PFLOP-day = $0.05-0.50 at modern cloud prices depending on precision.

snappy: Quadrillion (10¹⁵) math operations per second — the unit you use when talking about whole AI clusters.
detail: 1 PFLOPS = 1,000 TFLOPS. A single H100 GPU peaks around 1-2 PFLOPS at FP8. A frontier training cluster with thousands of GPUs reaches into the exaflop range (1,000+ PFLOPS). Used heavily in announcements about new AI supercomputers.

snappy: Splitting a model's layers across multiple GPUs — different GPUs hold different layers, examples flow through like an assembly line.
detail: Each batch is broken into micro-batches that move through the pipeline staggered, so all GPUs stay busy. Pipeline parallelism is necessary for very deep models and is often combined with tensor and data parallelism in a "3D parallel" setup. Megatron-LM and DeepSpeed are the standard libraries.

snappy: Extra information added to each token so the model knows where it sits in the sequence — Transformers have no built-in sense of word order.
detail: Without positional encoding a Transformer would see "dog bites man" and "man bites dog" as the same input. The original 2017 design used fixed sine-wave patterns. Modern models prefer learned variants like RoPE (rotary, used by Llama and GPT-NeoX) or ALiBi (linear bias, used by MPT). The choice affects how well a model generalises to longer contexts than it was trained on.

snappy: The reinforcement learning algorithm used inside RLHF — keeps updates small enough that the model doesn't drift away from its training.
detail: Developed by OpenAI in 2017, originally for video-game agents. PPO's "trust region" approach prevents the kind of overconfident gradient updates that destabilise RL training. In RLHF it's used to update the language model under guidance from the reward model. Newer alternatives like DPO try to avoid PPO's complexity, but PPO is still the workhorse for full RLHF pipelines.

snappy: The first big training phase where a model learns general language from huge amounts of internet text.
detail: A frontier LLM is first pretrained on trillions of tokens of text scraped from the internet — books, code, scientific papers, web pages. The model learns to predict the next word, which forces it to absorb grammar, facts, and patterns of reasoning. Pretraining is the most expensive step (millions of GPU-hours, hundreds of millions of dollars at the frontier) and produces the "base model" before any alignment work.

snappy: Server-side reuse of a common prompt prefix across many requests — pay the compute once, reuse it many times.
detail: If you hit the same model with the same long system prompt every time, prompt caching stores the KV cache for that prefix on the server. Subsequent requests skip recomputing it. OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google all expose prompt caching APIs with significant cost discounts (50–90%) for cached prefixes — important for any app that uses a long, stable system prompt.

snappy: Removing parameters from a trained model that contribute little to its output — shrinks size with small quality loss.
detail: Magnitude pruning zeroes out the smallest-weight connections. Structured pruning removes whole rows, columns, or attention heads to maintain hardware-friendly shapes. Pruning is less popular than quantization in modern LLM serving (quantization is more predictable) but is still important for edge deployment and embedded vision models.

snappy: LoRA combined with model quantisation, letting you fine-tune huge models on a single consumer GPU.
detail: Standard LoRA still needs the full model loaded in memory. QLoRA compresses (quantises) the frozen base model to 4 bits per parameter, dropping memory by 4-8×. The result: fine-tuning a 70B-parameter model fits on a single 24GB consumer GPU. Released by University of Washington researchers in 2023 and democratised LLM fine-tuning overnight.

snappy: Compressing a model by storing its numbers with fewer bits — smaller, faster, almost as accurate.
detail: Models are normally trained at 16- or 32-bit precision. Quantization rounds parameters down to 8, 4, or even 2 bits each, shrinking memory by 2-8× and speeding inference. Quality loss is small if done carefully. Quantised models can run on consumer hardware (phones, laptops) that couldn't otherwise host them. Standard formats: INT8, INT4, FP8.

snappy: Older type of language network that reads a sequence one word at a time, carrying a running memory between steps.
detail: RNNs dominated translation, speech recognition, and language modelling pre-2017. Their core weakness is that they have to process one word before the next — you can't parallelise across time — so training is slow on long sequences. Transformers replaced them for most tasks. State space models like Mamba are bringing some RNN-like ideas back, with a twist that makes them parallelisable.

snappy: Adversarial testing — having people deliberately try to break the model or get it to misbehave.
detail: Borrowed from cybersecurity. Red teams probe a model for jailbreaks, unsafe outputs, bias, hallucination, and other failure modes before release. Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google all run extensive red-team campaigns. Findings feed into refusal training and constitutional updates. The UK and US AI Safety Institutes do third-party red-teaming on frontier models before they ship.

snappy: Teaching a model when to say no — declining requests for help with harmful, illegal, or otherwise off-policy tasks.
detail: A subset of alignment training that produces the familiar "I can't help with that" responses to weapons synthesis, malware, or self-harm queries. Hard to get right: too liberal and the model is dangerous; too strict and it's annoying ("I can't tell you how to chop onions"). Modern refusal training is layered with explanation — the model says why it's declining and offers to help with the underlying need.

snappy: Downweighting tokens that have already appeared to stop the model getting stuck on the same words.
detail: Without it, language models tend to loop — "the the the the" or repeating phrases. The penalty multiplies the probability of recently-used tokens by a factor below 1 (typical 1.1-1.3). Heavier penalties harm fluency, lighter ones leave loops in. Largely solved by modern training but still an important inference-time knob for older or smaller models.

snappy: A trick that made it practical to train very deep networks — adds shortcut connections so signal can skip past layers.
detail: Before ResNets (Microsoft Research, 2015), deeper networks paradoxically performed worse than shallower ones. Adding identity shortcuts solved this and unlocked 100-plus-layer networks. The residual idea — add the input to the layer's output — is now everywhere: every Transformer block uses it. One of the most-copied design patterns in modern deep learning.

snappy: When a model finds shortcuts that score high on its reward signal but don't actually satisfy what the reward was meant to capture.
detail: Classic example: a robot trained to maximise points in a boat-racing game discovered it could rack up points by spinning in circles in a single area, ignoring the actual race. In RLHF, reward hacking shows up as models that game the reward model — producing outputs that look good to it but aren't actually helpful. A major reason RLHF needs careful design and many rounds of iteration.

snappy: A small AI model trained to predict what humans would prefer — used to score outputs during RLHF.
detail: Take pairs of model responses, ask humans which is better, train a model to predict those preferences. The reward model becomes a stand-in for human judgment during the RL phase, scoring each new output. Quality of the reward model is a major bottleneck on RLHF — a biased reward model bakes its biases into the final language model and is the main source of "reward hacking" failures.

snappy: RLHF variant where AI judges, not humans, score model outputs — much cheaper, almost as good.
detail: Human feedback is slow and expensive. RLAIF replaces some or all human raters with another AI model — typically a strong existing model used to rate or rank outputs. Anthropic's Constitutional AI is one form of RLAIF: the model critiques its own outputs against a written list of principles. Cheaper to scale, with the trade-off that you inherit whatever blind spots the judge model has.

snappy: Training technique where humans rate model outputs and the model learns to produce more of what's rated higher.
detail: First show humans pairs of responses to the same prompt and ask which they prefer. Train a reward model on those preferences. Then use reinforcement learning to nudge the language model toward higher reward. RLHF was the key step that turned GPT-3 into ChatGPT and underlies most consumer-grade LLMs. It's also where alignment researchers focus on bugs like reward hacking and sycophancy.

snappy: A diagram that shows whether a workload is bottlenecked by compute or by memory bandwidth, at a glance.
detail: Plot operations-per-byte (arithmetic intensity) on the x-axis, achieved throughput on the y-axis. The "roof" is a piecewise line: bandwidth-limited at low intensity, compute-limited at high intensity. The model — introduced by UC Berkeley — is the standard way of explaining why a particular kernel is slow and which way to fix it.

snappy: Reconfigurable dataflow accelerator from SambaNova — bet on a different architectural philosophy from GPUs.
detail: Each SN40L chip combines compute units with on-chip memory and reconfigurable interconnect. Targets enterprise customers who want to fine-tune and serve large models on dedicated hardware. Notable for very large model support (1T+ parameters) on small clusters.

snappy: Picking each next token randomly from the model's probability distribution, rather than always taking the top one.
detail: The umbrella over temperature, top-k, top-p, and similar decoding methods. Introduces controlled randomness that lets the model produce diverse, creative outputs instead of dull, deterministic ones. The main mechanism by which the same prompt to the same model gives slightly different answers each time.

snappy: Predictable performance gains from more compute, data, and parameters
detail: Empirical laws that relate model loss to compute, dataset size, and parameter count via power-law curves. Underpins frontier labs' strategy of investing in larger training runs on the bet that capabilities improve smoothly with scale. Kaplan et al. (2020) and the Chinchilla paper (2022) are the canonical references.

snappy: Attention used within a single piece of text — every word looks at every other word in the same input.
detail: Self-attention lets every word in a sequence consider every other word in the same sequence, all in one pass. This is what makes Transformers fast to train: they can process a whole sentence in parallel, instead of one word at a time like the older recurrent networks they replaced. It's the engine inside every modern large language model.

snappy: Hardware design where one instruction operates on a whole vector of numbers at once — the basic trick behind GPU parallelism.
detail: Instead of "add A to B", a SIMD machine does "add this list of 32 A's to this list of 32 B's, all at once". GPUs are massively SIMD: a single "warp" of 32 threads runs the same instruction across different data. CPUs have SIMD too (Intel's AVX, ARM's NEON) but on a much smaller scale. The single most important hardware concept for understanding why GPUs are fast.

snappy: Qualcomm's laptop-class chip with a strong NPU — the engine behind Microsoft Copilot+ PCs.
detail: Snapdragon X Elite (2024) was Qualcomm's serious entry into Windows laptops, with a 45 TOPS NPU dedicated to on-device AI. Microsoft requires 40+ TOPS for the Copilot+ branding, so Qualcomm, Intel (Lunar Lake), and AMD (Ryzen AI) all design chips with that in mind.

snappy: A complete computer (CPU + GPU + memory controllers + accelerators) packed onto a single chip.
detail: Phones and laptops are SoC-driven: Apple's M-series, Qualcomm's Snapdragon, Google's Tensor are all SoCs that combine CPU cores, GPU cores, an NPU, a media engine, and shared memory. SoCs win on power efficiency and physical size at the cost of upgradability — you can't swap the GPU on an iPhone.

snappy: The standard form of MoE today — for any given input, only a handful of the model's experts wake up.
detail: Contrasted with a "dense" version where every expert sees every input (rarely used in practice). The sparse routing has to be balanced carefully — otherwise a few experts get all the work and the rest never train. Google's Switch Transformer (2021) and GShard worked out the practical engineering. The payoff is dramatic: roughly 8× more capacity for the same inference cost.

snappy: Inference trick where a small model proposes several next tokens and the big model verifies them in parallel.
detail: Generating one token at a time is sequential and slow. A cheap "draft" model guesses the next 4-8 tokens; the expensive main model checks them all in a single batched forward pass; accepted guesses are kept and rejected ones trigger a fallback. Achieves 2-3× speedup on typical workloads. Used in production by most inference engines.

snappy: Fine-tuning a pretrained model on labeled examples of what the right answer looks like.
detail: SFT is typically the first step after pretraining. For a chat model, that means hundreds of thousands of human-written example conversations: prompt, ideal response. The model learns the format and style of helpful responses. SFT alone produces decent chatbots but leaves them prone to confident errors and unhelpful refusals — that's why RLHF or its alternatives come next.

snappy: When a model agrees with whatever the user says, even if it's wrong — a side effect of training on human preferences.
detail: RLHF rewards responses that humans rate highly. Humans tend to rate agreeable responses higher than challenging ones. The result: models that flatter, agree with mistaken claims, or change their answer when pushed even slightly. Recognised as a major alignment failure mode and the target of specific mitigation training. The 2023 Anthropic paper "Towards Understanding Sycophancy" documented the phenomenon in detail.

snappy: Knob that controls how random a model's output is — low values are predictable, high values are creative.
detail: Temperature scales the model's probability distribution before sampling. Temperature 0 is equivalent to greedy decoding. Temperature 0.7-1.0 is typical for chat. Above 1.5 the model starts to produce gibberish. The single most useful knob a developer can tune when integrating an LLM into a product.

snappy: NVIDIA's name for the matrix-multiplying units inside its GPUs — the part that does the AI heavy lifting.
detail: Introduced with the Volta architecture in 2017. A tensor core does a small matrix multiply (e.g., 4×4 × 4×4) in a single clock cycle, dramatically faster than doing it with regular CUDA cores. Modern H100s have hundreds of tensor cores per GPU and can do over 1,000 trillion operations per second when matrix-multiplying at FP8 precision.

snappy: Splitting a single layer's computation across multiple GPUs — needed when one layer doesn't fit on one chip.
detail: Each GPU holds part of the weight matrices and computes its slice in parallel; results are combined with high-bandwidth communication. Tensor parallelism is what makes frontier models (300B+ parameters) trainable and servable at all. Requires very fast interconnect — typically NVLink within a node, InfiniBand between nodes.

snappy: Trillion (10¹²) math operations per second — the standard unit for measuring chip compute throughput.
detail: An H100 hits about 1,000 TFLOPS at FP16, ~2,000 at FP8. A modern phone NPU does 30-50 TFLOPS. The metric is precision-dependent — a chip rated "1000 TFLOPS at FP8" might only do 500 at FP16 and 250 at FP32. Always check which precision is being quoted.

snappy: Total tokens (across all requests) a system produces per second — what matters for cost economics.
detail: While latency is about one user's experience, throughput is about how many users one GPU can serve. Continuous batching, large effective batch sizes, and quantisation all increase throughput. Modern H100s serve roughly 1,000-5,000 tokens/second per GPU depending on model size and quantisation; bigger systems aggregate this across many GPUs.

snappy: The atomic unit a language model reads or writes — usually a word fragment of 3–4 characters, not a full word.
detail: Models don't see characters or words directly; text is first split into tokens by a tokeniser. Common words like "the" become a single token; rare or compound words split into several. Context window is measured in tokens, API pricing is per-token, and a model's vocabulary size (typically 32k–256k) is the number of distinct tokens it knows.

snappy: Splitting text into tokens — the first preprocessing step before any model can read anything.
detail: Modern LLMs use subword tokenisers — algorithms that produce tokens shorter than words but longer than letters — balancing vocabulary size against token length. The tokeniser is part of the model contract: GPT-4 tokens and Llama tokens aren't interchangeable. Tokenisation quirks cause many oddities: weak arithmetic, struggles with character-level tasks, sensitivity to whitespace.

snappy: Decoding rule that restricts each step's choices to the K most likely tokens.
detail: Caps the long tail of the distribution: even if the model gives small probability to thousands of weird tokens, only the top K (typically 20-50) are considered. Combined with temperature and top-p to shape model output. A workhorse decoding rule throughout the 2020s, though top-p has largely replaced it in modern stacks.

snappy: Decoding rule that picks from however many tokens are needed to cover a probability mass of P.
detail: Instead of fixing the number of candidates (like top-k), top-p adapts: take the smallest set of tokens whose probabilities sum to P (typically 0.9-0.95). When the model is confident, this is a tiny set; when uncertain, it widens. Strikes a better balance than top-k in practice and is the default decoding strategy for most modern chat models.

snappy: Google's custom AI accelerator — designed from scratch for the matrix multiplies that dominate neural networks.
detail: Google has shipped six generations of TPUs since 2016. The hallmark is a systolic array: a grid of multipliers that pumps data through in a single rhythmic flow, very efficient for matrix multiplication. TPUs power Google search, Gemini training, and the Google Cloud AI offering. They're available only through Google Cloud, not for direct purchase.

snappy: Google's training-focused TPU generation, deployed in pods of thousands for Gemini training.
detail: Announced late 2023. 95GB of HBM, 459 TFLOPS BF16, connected via Google's custom optical interconnect into pods of up to 8,960 chips. Google trained Gemini Ultra on TPU v5p. The TPU v5e is a smaller, cheaper variant for inference. Followed by Trillium (v6) in 2024 and Ironwood in 2025.

snappy: Amazon's custom training accelerator — AWS's bet on owning the AI stack vertically.
detail: Trainium 1 launched 2022. Trainium 2 (2024) targets H100-class performance at lower price-per-FLOP for AWS-internal workloads. Used by Anthropic for some Claude training (Anthropic has a deep AWS partnership). Available only through AWS, not for direct purchase.

snappy: Sequence model based on self-attention
detail: Architecture introduced by Vaswani et al. (2017) that replaced recurrent networks for sequence modeling. Self-attention lets every token attend to every other token in a context window, enabling massive parallelism in training. Underlies GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, and essentially every modern large language model.

snappy: Google's 2024 TPU generation — first major step beyond the v5 line.
detail: Roughly 4.7× the compute of v5e at the same power. Used for Gemini 2 training and inference. Like all TPUs, only available inside Google Cloud — you can't buy one. The successor Ironwood (announced 2025) is positioned as an inference-optimised generation with focus on serving large models efficiently.

snappy: Image-to-image network shaped like a U — compresses the picture down, then expands it back up.
detail: Designed for biomedical image segmentation in 2015. The encoder downsamples, the decoder upsamples, and shortcut connections pass high-resolution detail across the U. U-Net is now the workhorse of diffusion models — Stable Diffusion's denoising network is a U-Net — making it one of the most-used architectures by FLOP-hours in the world.

snappy: Model that learns to squeeze data down to a small code and reconstruct it — the workhorse compressor inside many image generators.
detail: VAEs are trained to compress and rebuild data, with the constraint that the compressed code lives in a smooth, well-behaved space you can sample from. By themselves they produce slightly blurry samples. But they're now essential infrastructure inside latent diffusion: the part that turns pixels into compressed representations before diffusion, and back again afterwards.

snappy: Transformer applied to images by chopping them into small patches and treating each patch like a word.
detail: Introduced by Google in 2020. Split an image into 16×16 patches, project each into an embedding, then run a standard Transformer encoder. ViTs match or beat CNNs at scale, especially when pretrained on enormous datasets. The architecture underpins modern vision-language models — CLIP, GPT-4o's vision capability, Gemini's image understanding — and most image generators.

snappy: An accelerator that's a single chip the size of an entire silicon wafer — Cerebras's distinctive design.
detail: Most chips are diced from a wafer into hundreds of small pieces. Cerebras keeps the whole wafer intact, yielding a single chip with ~900,000 cores and 44GB of on-chip memory. No off-chip memory bandwidth limits, no chip-to-chip communication overhead. Trade-off: yield is harder (one defect can kill the whole wafer) and they need custom packaging and cooling.

snappy: Cerebras's flagship — a single chip the size of a dinner plate with 900,000 cores.
detail: Latest of three wafer-scale generations. 4 trillion transistors, 44GB of on-chip SRAM, 21 PB/s of memory bandwidth (orders of magnitude more than any GPU). Used by Cerebras's inference cloud, which posts world-record latency numbers for large model serving by keeping everything on-chip.

snappy: Microsoft's approach to sharding optimizer state, gradients, and parameters across GPUs — the original sharded-data-parallel design.
detail: Three "stages" of sharding: ZeRO-1 shards optimizer state only; ZeRO-2 adds gradients; ZeRO-3 adds parameters (equivalent to PyTorch's FSDP). Implemented in Microsoft's DeepSpeed library. Single most important technique for training frontier-scale models on clusters of hundreds or thousands of GPUs.
